text: The surgeon carefully dissects along the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, ensuring precise separation of the nerve from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the dissection through the space between the esophagus and trachea, utilizing the pre-esophageal approach to access the surgical site.
The surgeon divides the soft tissue between the esophagus and trachea, utilizing the pre-esophageal approach to access the surgical area.
The surgeon exposes the left main bronchus, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully controls small vascular branches in the surgical field, using precise techniques to manage these vessels during the esophagectomy.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, aiming to reach as high as possible on the right side, focusing on the pre-esophageal approach between the esophagus and trachea.
The surgeon points out a visible knob, which may be the recurrent laryngeal nerve or a sympathetic nerve, located near the esophagus.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field towards the neck, following the right side approach, with visible anatomical landmarks guiding the dissection.
The surgical field shows a structure that may be the thyroid gland, located in the upper region of the neck.
The surgeon identifies a bleeding vessel, pointing it out within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the robotic arm to suture the surface of the esophagus to the thoracic wall, securing it to the chest wall.
The surgeon performs robotic suturing, tying, and tagging at two or three points within the surgical field, demonstrating the ease of these procedures with robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses the third arm to dissect and mobilize tissue around the trachea, holding the left side of the trachea in the posterior membrane without affecting ventilation.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create space and divide tissue, progressing upwards towards a holding point.

During the dissection in the upper area, the surgeon carefully navigates near the trachea, where the nerve is closely situated. The surgeon is cautious to avoid the recurrent laryngeal nerve, which is not currently visible.

The surgeon controls small branches of tissue, attempting to identify the recurrent laryngeal nerve and associated nodes. The surgeon points out a structure believed to be the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon examines two visible knobs, attempting to differentiate between the recurrent laryngeal nerve and another syn-passing knob, highlighting the importance of accurate identification during the procedure.

The surgeon cuts the nodal tissue, following the knob to the distal area to identify the recurrent rhizome. The recurrent portion of the knob is visible, along with the symposium extending to the specified area.

The surgeon identifies the bronchiolatory region, noting the importance of preserving the left bronchial artery to prevent ischemia in the airway. Careful control of lymph nodes is emphasized in this area.
The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery, which is surrounded by a significant amount of blood, indicating active bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon aims to remove all lymph nodes surrounding the pulmonary artery and main bronchus, focusing on the anatomical area visible in the surgical field.

Following the removal of lymph nodes, the surgeon demonstrates the preserved vessels and knob, ensuring no damage to the pulmonary artery. The procedure progresses to the upper area with the trachea being held.

The surgeon performs node dissection along the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, carefully removing only the lymphatic tissue between the esophagus and the nerve while preserving the perineural tissue to avoid ischemia.

The surgeon navigates around the recurrent laryngeal nerve, encountering multiple small branches. The complexity of controlling these branches is visible, as the energy device is challenging to use in this area.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of an energy device, maintaining a safe distance of three to five millimeters from the visible nerve to prevent damage, as the nerve is clearly identifiable in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the thyroid gland in the surgical field, indicating the procedure has reached the neck region. The entire tissue below the thyroid is to be removed, and the left cervical parasympathetic lymph node is visible nearby.
The surgeon removes additional lymphatic and fatty tissue in the lower neck, carefully controlling vascular branches during the process.
The surgeon cuts a specific tissue or structure in the surgical field, using an instrument to make the incision.

The finalized view after the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection shows the aortic arch, recurrent laryngeal nerve, sympathetic nerve, and thyroid gland. The surrounding tissue around the thyroid gland and recurrent laryngeal nerve has been removed, revealing the left hilum and other anatomical structures.
